sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage:
A cancer biology lab studies cells exposed to ultraviolet light. After exposure, sequencing reveals clustered C→T transitions at adjacent pyrimidines. A repair-deficient cell line accumulates bulky helix-distorting lesions and shows high sensitivity to UV. Introducing a wild-type repair protein restores survival.

Cell line | UV lesions removed after 6 h | Survival after UV | Mutation burden
Wild type | high | 80% | low
Repair deficient | low | 20% | high
Rescued | high | 76% | low

The investigators compare nucleotide excision repair, base excision repair, mismatch repair, and homologous recombination. They note that UV commonly produces pyrimidine dimers, whereas oxidative deamination and small abnormal bases are often handled by glycosylase-initiated pathways.
questions.stem: Which repair pathway most directly removes UV-induced pyrimidine dimers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nonhomologous end joining

choices.label: B
choices.text: Base excision repair only

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mismatch repair after replication only

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nucleotide excision repair

explanations.A: Nonhomologous end joining repairs double-strand breaks by ligating DNA ends. UV dimers are covalent links between adjacent pyrimidines, not broken chromosome ends.
explanations.B: Base excision repair is more typical for small base lesions such as uracil from deamination or oxidized bases. It is not the main pathway for bulky UV dimers that distort the helix.
explanations.C: Mismatch repair corrects replication errors such as mispaired bases or small insertion-deletion loops. It does not primarily recognize the original bulky UV photoproduct before replication.
explanations.D: Correct. Nucleotide excision repair removes bulky, helix-distorting lesions such as UV-induced pyrimidine dimers by cutting out a short damaged strand segment and filling it in using the undamaged strand as template.

questions.stem: Why does the rescue experiment strengthen the causal claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Restoring the wild-type protein restores lesion removal and survival.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It proves UV creates no DNA damage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It shows all mutations are beneficial.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It eliminates the need for controls.

explanations.A: Correct. The deficient cells have poor lesion removal and low survival, while adding back the wild-type repair protein restores both. That pattern links the phenotype specifically to loss of the repair function rather than unrelated background differences.
explanations.B: UV damage is central to the experiment; the wild-type and rescued cells survive better because lesions are removed, not because damage never forms.
explanations.C: The passage shows high mutation burden is associated with repair deficiency and low survival. There is no evidence that the mutations are beneficial.
explanations.D: Rescue experiments are powerful because they complement controls; they do not replace them. Wild type, deficient, and rescued comparisons together support specificity.

questions.stem: Which lesion would more likely be handled by base excision repair?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A bulky thymine dimer

choices.label: B
choices.text: A chromosome broken into two ends

choices.label: C
choices.text: Uracil generated by cytosine deamination

choices.label: D
choices.text: A whole missing chromosome

explanations.A: A bulky thymine dimer is the classic nucleotide excision repair substrate in the passage. It distorts the helix and requires removal of an oligonucleotide patch.
explanations.B: Broken DNA ends are repaired by double-strand break pathways such as nonhomologous end joining or homologous recombination, not glycosylase-initiated base excision.
explanations.C: Correct. Base excision repair begins with DNA glycosylases that recognize small abnormal bases, such as uracil in DNA after cytosine deamination. The base is removed, then the sugar-phosphate backbone is repaired.
explanations.D: A missing chromosome is a large-scale segregation or chromosomal abnormality, not a single damaged base. Base excision repair operates at the molecular lesion level.

questions.stem: If UV lesions are not repaired before replication, why can mutations arise?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ribosomes directly copy pyrimidine dimers into protein.

choices.label: B
choices.text: DNA polymerase may insert incorrect bases opposite damaged templates.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Histones become nucleotides.

choices.label: D
choices.text: RNA polymerase permanently removes all exons.

explanations.A: Ribosomes translate mRNA; they do not copy DNA lesions into protein. Mutation fixation occurs during DNA replication, not translation.
explanations.B: Correct. Unrepaired lesions can block or distort replication, and translesion synthesis or mispairing can insert incorrect bases. Once a wrong base is copied in a later round, the change can become a fixed mutation.
explanations.C: Histones package DNA but do not become nucleotides. Chromatin state can affect repair access, but histones are not converted into DNA bases.
explanations.D: RNA polymerase transcribes genes and can stall at lesions, but it does not permanently remove all exons. Exon removal is part of RNA splicing, not UV mutagenesis.

questions.stem: The C→T transitions at adjacent pyrimidines are most consistent with:

choices.label: A
choices.text: UV signature mutations after pyrimidine dimer formation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: exclusive mitochondrial inheritance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: translation initiation failure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: loss of all DNA methylation marks.

explanations.A: Correct. UV exposure commonly produces cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers and related lesions, leading to characteristic C→T transitions, especially at dipyrimidine sites, when damage is misrepaired or bypassed.
explanations.B: Mitochondrial inheritance concerns transmission of mitochondrial DNA through the maternal line. It does not explain clustered nuclear UV-signature transitions.
explanations.C: Translation initiation failure affects protein synthesis from mRNA. It would not create a specific DNA mutation pattern at adjacent pyrimidines.
explanations.D: DNA methylation changes can regulate gene expression and mutation risk at CpG sites, but the passage's adjacent-pyrimidine UV signature specifically points to UV photoproducts.

questions.passage:
A developmental biology group studies a gene that is silent in stem cells but active after differentiation. Chromatin immunoprecipitation shows high histone acetylation and low promoter DNA methylation after differentiation. Treatment with a histone deacetylase inhibitor activates the gene partially even before differentiation.

Condition | Promoter DNA methylation | Histone acetylation | mRNA level
Stem cells | high | low | low
Differentiated | low | high | high
HDAC inhibitor | high | medium | medium

The group emphasizes that epigenetic marks affect chromatin accessibility without changing the underlying DNA sequence. They also distinguish promoter methylation from gene-body methylation and note that transcription factors still need accessible binding sites.
questions.stem: Why is histone acetylation associated with increased transcription in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acetylation converts RNA into DNA.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acetylation deletes the promoter sequence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acetylation neutralizes lysine positive charge, loosening histone-DNA interactions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acetylation prevents all transcription factor binding.

explanations.A: RNA-to-DNA conversion is reverse transcription, not histone acetylation. Histone acetylation modifies chromatin proteins, not nucleic acid directionality.
explanations.B: Epigenetic modification does not delete promoter DNA. The passage explicitly says epigenetic marks affect accessibility without changing the DNA sequence.
explanations.C: Correct. Histone acetylation reduces the positive charge on lysine residues, weakening interactions with negatively charged DNA and making chromatin more accessible. This can facilitate transcription-factor binding and RNA polymerase recruitment.
explanations.D: The passage links acetylation to activation, so preventing all transcription-factor binding would predict lower mRNA. Acetylation generally increases accessibility, though transcription factors are still required.

questions.stem: High promoter DNA methylation most commonly correlates with:

choices.label: A
choices.text: ribosome assembly on DNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: increased promoter accessibility in all cases

choices.label: C
choices.text: changing the DNA sequence from A to G

choices.label: D
choices.text: transcriptional repression

explanations.A: Ribosomes translate mRNA in the cytoplasm or rough ER; they do not assemble on DNA promoters. This option confuses transcriptional regulation with translation.
explanations.B: The passage shows the opposite pattern: low promoter methylation accompanies high mRNA after differentiation. While context matters, high promoter methylation is classically repressive.
explanations.C: DNA methylation adds a methyl group, commonly to cytosine, but it does not change the base sequence. A sequence mutation would be genetic, not epigenetic.
explanations.D: Correct. Promoter CpG methylation often recruits repressive complexes or blocks transcription-factor binding, lowering transcription. In the table, high promoter methylation in stem cells corresponds to low mRNA.

questions.stem: Why does the HDAC inhibitor only partially activate the gene?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It removes all DNA from the nucleus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It increases acetylation but promoter methylation and missing differentiation factors may still limit transcription.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It guarantees maximal transcription of every gene.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It mutates the coding sequence into an intron.

explanations.A: HDAC inhibitors alter chromatin acetylation; they do not remove nuclear DNA. If DNA were removed, transcription would cease rather than rise to medium levels.
explanations.B: Correct. HDAC inhibition raises histone acetylation, improving accessibility, but the promoter remains highly methylated and differentiation-specific transcription factors may be absent. Gene expression depends on multiple layers of regulation, so one epigenetic change may produce only partial activation.
explanations.C: The table contradicts maximal transcription: mRNA is medium, not high. HDAC inhibition can activate some genes but does not override every regulatory requirement.
explanations.D: Changing coding sequence into an intron would be a genetic or splicing-related alteration, not the mechanism of a deacetylase inhibitor. The passage emphasizes unchanged DNA sequence.

questions.stem: Which statement best distinguishes epigenetic regulation from mutation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Epigenetic regulation changes gene expression without changing DNA sequence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Epigenetic regulation always changes codons.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mutation never affects phenotype.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Epigenetic marks cannot be copied during cell division.

explanations.A: Correct. Epigenetic marks such as DNA methylation and histone modifications influence chromatin accessibility and transcription while preserving the nucleotide sequence. Mutations alter the sequence itself.
explanations.B: Changing codons is a sequence-level mutation, not epigenetic regulation. Epigenetic changes can alter how much transcript is made without rewriting codons.
explanations.C: Mutations can certainly affect phenotype by changing protein sequence, regulation, splicing, or chromosome structure. The distinction is sequence change, not phenotypic relevance.
explanations.D: Some epigenetic marks can be maintained through cell division, contributing to cellular memory. Maintenance is not perfect or universal, but saying they cannot be copied is too absolute.

questions.stem: A transcription factor fails to bind in stem cells despite being present. Which passage detail best explains this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mRNA level is already high.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The gene has no DNA sequence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: High promoter methylation and low acetylation reduce chromatin accessibility.

choices.label: D
choices.text: HDAC inhibitor removes transcription factors from the nucleus.

explanations.A: The stem-cell mRNA level is low, not high. Low expression is consistent with inaccessible chromatin and poor transcription-factor binding.
explanations.B: The gene must have DNA sequence if it can later be transcribed after differentiation. Epigenetic silencing does not mean the gene is physically absent.
explanations.C: Correct. In stem cells, the promoter is highly methylated and histone acetylation is low, a combination associated with closed chromatin. Even if a transcription factor is present, it may not access its binding site effectively.
explanations.D: The passage says HDAC inhibitor partially activates the gene; it does not remove transcription factors from the nucleus. This answer invents a drug effect not described.
